primary_modes.rationale: The task is entirely about component-library naming conventions, prop ergonomics, and enforced correctness through TypeScript/prop-types — all hallmarks of Developer Experience work on a shared UI toolkit.

secondary_modes.rationale: REQ-0014 (required alt on images, required label on buttons) directly maps to accessibility requirements, which is a UX/a11y concern even though it is expressed as a DX constraint.
If new components (OiTable, OiSidebar, etc.) need to be scaffolded or renamed from existing ones, this crosses into feature/component development work.

generic.rationale: Anchors why inconsistent naming and loose prop contracts are a problem worth solving right now.
Explicitly state that the goal is a consistent, readable, self-documenting component API across the Oi* namespace.
Clarify scope — e.g. this ticket does NOT redesign component behaviour, only naming and prop signatures.
Capture assumptions such as: TypeScript is the enforcement mechanism, an 'Oi' prefix is the agreed namespace, breaking changes are acceptable in this phase.
All project checks (format, analyze, test) must pass — this is a hard gate.
Already provided; must be translated into verifiable exit criteria for each atom (REQ-0012, REQ-0013, REQ-0014).
Per-atom, per-component acceptance criteria are needed to validate the rename + prop changes are correct and complete.
Type-level tests (e.g. expect TypeScript to error when alt is missing) and unit tests for prop contract enforcement are required.
Breaking changes to existing consumers of the library must be risk-assessed; codemods or deprecation paths may be needed.
If consuming apps cannot immediately migrate, a backward-compat shim or deprecation alias strategy should be defined.

specialized.rationale: Core deliverable — prop naming conventions (multiSelect, stickyHeader) and component namespace (OiTable) are pure developer-ergonomics decisions.
The Oi* component library is effectively an internal SDK; the naming and required-prop rules define its public contract.
A codemod CLI (e.g. jscodeshift) to rename components/props across consuming codebases would be valuable but is not strictly required for Part 1.
REQ-0014 mandates alt on images and label on buttons — these are WCAG-level accessibility requirements expressed as enforced prop contracts.

recommended_specialist_agents.rationale: Owns the component naming standard, prop ergonomics design, and enforcement mechanism (TypeScript strict types, ESLint rules).
Must sign off on the Oi* namespace convention, breaking-change strategy, and prop API design across the component tree.
Responsible for type-level and unit test coverage of required props and verifying coverage ledger updates for REQ-0012–0014.
Should validate that required accessibility props (alt, label) align with the broader a11y strategy and WCAG targets.
Naming conventions must be documented in the component library's contributing guide and storybook so future components follow the same standard.

assumptions: TypeScript is the primary enforcement mechanism for required props (strict compile-time errors).
The 'Oi' prefix is the agreed, locked namespace for all components in this library.
This is a greenfield or early-stage library where breaking renames are acceptable without a long deprecation window.
All three atoms (REQ-0012, REQ-0013, REQ-0014) are in scope for Part 1; Part 2 will handle further convention work.
A coverage ledger (e.g. a traceability matrix or test-coverage report tagged by REQ ID) already exists and needs updating.
Existing consumers (if any) will receive a migration guide or codemod rather than indefinite backward-compat aliases.

open_questions.question: Are there existing components that must be renamed (breaking change), or is this greenfield naming for net-new components only?
open_questions.why_it_matters: Determines whether a codemod, deprecation shim, or major-version bump is required alongside this work.
open_questions.suggested_default_if_unknown: Assume some existing components exist; plan for a codemod script and a single major-version bump.

open_questions.question: What is the enforcement mechanism for prop naming conventions — TypeScript types only, ESLint custom rules, or both?
open_questions.why_it_matters: ESLint rules catch runtime-JS consumers; TypeScript only catches TS consumers. The team's stack determines coverage.
open_questions.suggested_default_if_unknown: TypeScript strict interfaces as primary enforcement; ESLint plugin-react prop-types as secondary.

open_questions.question: Is 'label' on buttons meant to replace or supplement the existing children prop, and how does this interact with icon-only buttons?
open_questions.why_it_matters: A required label prop that conflicts with children renders existing button usages invalid and may block icon-only patterns.
open_questions.suggested_default_if_unknown: label is an accessible text string (maps to aria-label for icon-only buttons); children remains the visual slot.

open_questions.question: Which WCAG level (A, AA, AAA) does the required alt/label enforcement target?
open_questions.why_it_matters: Determines whether additional attributes (e.g. aria-describedby, role) also need to be required props in this batch.
open_questions.suggested_default_if_unknown: WCAG 2.1 AA as the baseline compliance target.

open_questions.question: Does the coverage ledger update need to be automated (CI gate) or is a manual update acceptable for Part 1?
open_questions.why_it_matters: Automated coverage tracing is more reliable but adds CI complexity; manual is faster but error-prone.
open_questions.suggested_default_if_unknown: Manual update acceptable for Part 1; automation deferred to Part 2 or a dedicated infra ticket.
